insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution. This protocol settles verified purchase events at roughly $0.10 each — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't a negotiating point, it's a structural difference in what's being sold.

No pixel, no lookback window, no modeled conversion — the receipt is the cryptographically signed confirmation that a purchase occurred. For brands running CPA campaigns, this eliminates the attribution layer entirely rather than improving it.

insights.topic: Cookie deprecation creates the entry point
insights.insight: Chrome has joined Safari and Firefox in killing cross-site cookies, and device fingerprinting is being closed off by regulation and browser privacy updates. Brands dependent on third-party probabilistic targeting have no clean replacement inside the existing ad tech stack.

insights.topic: Cross-ecosystem coverage that platforms can't replicate
insights.insight: Google, Apple, Stripe, and Microsoft each see purchase signals only inside their own rails — all identity-attached, all siloed. A Stripe receipt and an Apple receipt processed through this protocol produce the same spend token, making it the only cross-ecosystem verified purchase layer currently visible.

Verified receipts are now appearing in rural Massachusetts and across a Northeast non-metro arc including Maine, Maryland, New York, and Pennsylvania — markets where probabilistic ad tech has the weakest data quality and lowest penetration, making verified signals from those regions proportionally scarcer and more valuable.

Fetch and Ibotta hold real purchase data, but it's identity-attached and not queryable by third-party brands. The data exists; the access doesn't. Any brand that has tried to license consumer receipt data at scale has already run into this wall.
